coredata.dc:title: Direct encapsulation of AIE-active dye with β cyclodextrin terminated polymers: Self-assembly and biological imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Hongye
Xu, Dazhuang
Liu, Meiying
Jiang, Ruming
Mao, Liucheng
Huang, Qiang
Wan, Qing
Wen, Yuanqing
Zhang, Xiaoyong
Wei, Yen
coredata.dc:description: Aggregation-induced emission (AIE) phenomenon has attracted great attention recently and been extensively explored for biomedical applications. Nevertheless, the direct utilization of AIE-active dyes for biomedical applications has demonstrated to be enormous challenge owing to the hydrophobic nature of these AIE-active dyes. In this work, we reported the fabrication of amphiphilic AIE-active copolymers through the specific host-guest interaction between β cyclodextrin (β-CD) and an adamantine terminating tetraphenylethene derivative (TPE-Ad). In this construction system, β-CD was acted as the bridge to link TPE-Ad with PEG. The TPE-β-CD-PEG copolymers were characterized by various equipments in detail. Cytocompatibility and cell uptake behavior of TPE-β-CD-PEG were also examined to evaluate their biomedical application potential. Results demonstrated that TPE-β-CD-PEG copolymers were prone to self-assemble into luminescent nanoparticles, which exhibited high water dispersity, AIE feature and excellent biocompatibility. These features endowed TPE-β-CD-PEG great potential for biomedical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aggregation-induced emission
Fluorescent organic nanoparticles
Self-assembly
Supramolecular interactions
Biological imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cell imaging using confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The 1H NMR spectra of the TPE-β-CD-PEG, β-CD-PEG, TPE-Ad. The chemical shift at 1.5–2ppm could be ascribed to the Ad. Furthermore the chemical shift appeared between 7.5 and 8.2ppm was the peaks of Benzene and the peaks of 3–5ppm could be ascribed to β-CD and PEG.

Normalized FT-IR spectra of TPE-Ad, β-CD-PEG and TPE-β-CD-PEG samples, strong stretching vibration bands of OH which located at 3500cm−1, the peaks of CH2 which appeared at 2900cm−1, the peaks of CO which located at 1700cm−1 and CO stretching vibration bands which located at 1100cm−1 were observed in the samples. The new peaks of benzene ring which located at 600cm−1 in TPE-β-CD-PEG samples, suggested that TPE-β-CD-PEG FONs were formed.
UV–Vis spectrum of TPE-β-CD-PEG. The inset is the visible image of TPE-β-CD-PEG in water using the logo of “AIE” symbol as background.
Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of TPE-β-CD-PEG, the inset is the fluorescence image of TPE-β-CD-PEG taken at 365nm UV light.
Cell viability of TPE-β-CD-PEG FONs with cells for 8 and 24h. The A549 cells were incubated with different concentrations (10–100μgmL−1) of TPE-β-CD-PEG FONs.
CLSM images of TPE-β-CD-PEG FONs with A549 cells. Cells were incubated with 30μgmL−1 of TPE-β-CD-PEG FONs for 3h. (A) fluorescent image, (B) bright fields, (C) merged image of A and B.

Schematic showing the preparation of fluorescent organic nanoparticles (FONs) (TPE-β-CD-PEG) that relied on the formation of specific orthogonal assembly between β-CD and Ad. The detailed procedure for the synthesis of TPE-Ad and β-CD-PEG was listed in the supporting information.

head.ce:title: Direct encapsulation of AIE-active dye with β cyclodextrin terminated polymers: Self-assembly and biological imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Nanchang University, 999 Xuefu Avenue, Nanchang 330031, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and the Tsinghua Center for Frontier Polymer Research, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and the Tsinghua Center for Frontier Polymer Research

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Aggregation-induced emission (AIE) phenomenon has attracted great attention recently and been extensively explored for biomedical applications. Nevertheless, the direct utilization of AIE-active dyes for biomedical applications has demonstrated to be enormous challenge owing to the hydrophobic nature of these AIE-active dyes. In this work, we reported the fabrication of amphiphilic AIE-active copolymers through the specific host-guest interaction between β cyclodextrin (β-CD) and an adamantine terminating tetraphenylethene derivative (TPE-Ad). In this construction system, β-CD was acted as the bridge to link TPE-Ad with PEG. The TPE-β-CD-PEG copolymers were characterized by various equipments in detail. Cytocompatibility and cell uptake behavior of TPE-β-CD-PEG were also examined to evaluate their biomedical application potential. Results demonstrated that TPE-β-CD-PEG copolymers were prone to self-assemble into luminescent nanoparticles, which exhibited high water dispersity, AIE feature and excellent biocompatibility. These features endowed TPE-β-CD-PEG great potential for biomedical applications.

Fluorescent organic nanoparticles with aggregation-induced emission feature were facilely prepared through the host-guest interaction between the amantadine terminating dye and β-CD functionalized PEG.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Since the first definition of aggregation-induced emission (AIE) by Tang et al. in 2001, this abnormal phenomenon has attracted increasing research interest [1–3]. The mechanism of AIE phenomenon is ascribed to the restriction of intramolecular rotation of AIE molecules within aggregates [4–6]. Therefore, being different from traditional organic dyes, AIE-active dyes are weak-emissive in the dissolved states but emit enhanced fluorescence in their aggregated states. It could overcome the notorious aggregation caused quenching (ACQ) effect, which limited usage of organic dyes for practical applications [7–9]. In view of the unique property of AIE-active dyes, it had great potential applications in bio/chemsensors, theranostic, organic light-emitting diodes (OLEDs) and bioimaging [4,10–12]. Especially, fabricating fluorescent organic nanoparticles (FONs) based on AIE-active dyes for bioimaging applications have been extensively pursued very recently [13–16]. The FONs based on AIE-active dyes are expected to effectively conquer the ACQ effect of conventional organic dyes. Nevertheless the hydrophobic nature of AIE-active dyes was an enormous challenge for the direct biomedical applications of these AIE-active materials [17–19]. Considerable efforts have been devoted to fabricating AIE-active FONs through different strategies [20–24]. The well known strategies such as surfactant encapsulation, revisable addition fragmentation chain transfer polymerization, emulsion polymerization, ring-opening polymerization, multicomponent reactions and the formation of dynamic bonds [10,12,23-27,30–32]. To the best of our knowledge, the fabrication of AIE-active FONs through supramolecular interaction has rarely reported thus far.

β cyclodextrin (β-CD) that represents a novel supramolecular macrocycles, has been applied widely in materials science and supramolecular chemistry due to its stable structures, facile functionalization and distinctive host–guest properties [33–46]. Different multifunctional and stimuli-responsive materials have been fabricated based on the supramolecular complexes of β-CD. For example, electrical response micelles have been fabricated and achieved by Yuan et al. [47]. They demonstrated that the β-CD and ferrocene inclusion complexes could be adjusted by the extra voltage. However, to the best of our knowledge, the fabrication of AIE-active FONs through the host-guest interaction has been rarely reported.

In this contribution, we reported the fabrication of AIE-active FONs through a facile one-pot supramolecular chemistry, in which the adamantine terminating tetraphenylethylenes (TPE-Ad) was encapsulated by β-CD functionalized polyethylene glycol (PEG) through formation of inclusion complexes. The synthetic process could be clearly described in Scheme 1 . Firstly, we synthesized a new AIE-active dye with adamantane (TPE-Ad) through the procedure in Fig. S1. Secondly, the β-CD functionalized by PEG (β-CD-PEG) was obtained via the conjugation of β-CD-NH2 with anhydride containing PEG. The detailed procedure for preparation of β-CD-PEG was displayed in supplementary information (Fig. S2). Then, β-CD-PEG could form an exact 1:1 inclusion complex with TPE-Ad via host-guest interaction and the TPE-β-CD-PEG FONs with excellent properties could be prepared by via self-assembly. Finally, we would further explore the potential of TPE-β-CD-PEG FONs in bioimaging application.

All chemical agents were of analytical grade and used as received without any further purification. All aqueous solutions were prepared with doubly distilled water. All the chemicals such as 4-Formylphenylboronic (M W: 149.94Da, 98%), ethylenediamine (M W: 60.1Da, 98%) Tosyl chloride (M W: 190.7Da, 98%), Bromotriphenylethylene (M W: 335.2Da, 98%), Triethyl phosphite (M W: 166.2Da, 98%), 1-Adamantyl bromomethyl ketone (M W: 257.2Da, 97%), β-Cyclodextrin (M W: 1134.4Da, 98%) Polyethylene glycol (PEG, M W: 2000Da) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical reagents Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

The synthetic polymers and materials were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) using KBr pellets, the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were supplied from Nicolet5700 (Thermo Nicolet corporation). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded on a Hitachi 7650B microscope operated at 80kV; the TEM specimens were got by putting a drop of the nanoparticle ethanol suspension on a carbon-coated copper grid. 1H and 13C NMR spectra were recorded on Bruker Avance-400 spectrometer with D2O, DMSO and CDCl3 as the solvents. Mass spectra (MS) were recorded on Trip TOFTM 5600. The fluorescence data were obtained from the Fluorescence spectrophotometer (FSP, model: C11367-11), which purchased from Hamamatsu (Japan). The size distribution and zeta potential of TPE-β-CD-PEG was determined using a Zeta Plus apparatus (ZetaPlus, Brookhaven Instruments, Holtsville, NY).

The 1-Adamantyl bromomethyl ketone (848mg, 3.3mmol) was mixed with triethyl phosphate (10mL), and resulting mixture was refluxed at 180°C for 8h. In order to obtain the pure product, the solvent should be removed at vacuum and the residue product was purified by a column chromatography on silica gel using ethyl acetate/petroleum ether as the eluent. Yield: 925mg (68%).1H NMR (300MHz, CDCl3): δ 3.98 (m, 4H), δ 1.92 (t, 3H), δ 1.67 (d, 6H), δ 1.55 (d, 6H), δ 1.18 (t, 6H).

4-(aldehyde) Tetraphenylethylenes (TPE-CHO) was synthesized according to our previous report [12]. Then, TPE-CHO (425mg, 1.22mmol) Ad-PO(OET)2 (500mg, 1.22mmol) and t-BuOK (224mg, 2mmol) in dry THF solution were stirred at 40°C for 5h. The THF solution was removed by rotary evaporation and the crude product was separated by column chromatography on silica gel using ethyl acetate /CH2Cl2 (1:3) as the eluent. Yield: 532mg (58%). 1H NMR (300MHz, CDCl3): δ 7.55(m, 2H) δ 7.26(m, 14H) δ 7.04(m, 3H) δ 2.04(s, 3H) δ 1.83(d, 6H) δ 1.65 (m, 6H).

β-CD (10g, 8.8mmol) and Tosyl chloride (3.36g, 17.36mmol) were dissolved in 200mL NaOH solution (0.75molL−1) and stirred at 0°C for 5h. Then, adjust the pH to 7 by hydrochloric acid and the intermediate products could be obtained by filtration. After dried for 12h, the intermediate products (5g) were stirred in 30mL ethylenediamine at 75°C for 4h. Finally, acetone was added into the solution and the product (β-CD-NH2) was obtained by filtration.

The PEG (1mmol, 2g) were dissolved in the 20mL anhydrous THF solution in a dried flask. Then, drops of triethylamine solution and trimellitic anhydride chloride (2.5mmol, 526mg) in 10mL dry THF solution was added dropwise into reaction system at room temperature (20°C). After 10min, added β-CD-NH2 (500mg) to flask and stirred for 1h. Next, the solution was purified by dialysis in water for 1 d. The final product (β-CD-PEG) could be prepared by filtration [48].

The synthetic TPE-Ad (50mg) and synthetic β-CD-PEG (250mg) dissolved in DMSO solution, which only need stirring at room temperature for 30s. Then, by-product and reactant were removed via dialysis in water. Finally, the product (TPE-β-CD-PEG) was separated by centrifugation, and was purified by washing with deionized water three times to obtain free β-CD.

The determination of cell viability of xenobiotics or biomaterials is very important for evaluation their potential in biomedical fields. Here, the cell viability values of TPE-β-CD-PEG FONs were determined using a well known assay named as cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) using A549 cells [49–51]. The details about the CCK-8 were shown in the supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell imaging using confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM)
ce:section.ce:para: The cell uptake behavior of TPE-β-CD-PEG FONs was conducted on A549 cells to evaluate their biological imaging potential. Detailed experimental procedures were listed in the supporting information.

The biomedical applications of fluorescent nanomaterials for biomedical applications have attracted great research attention for their small size, high photostability, multifunctional potential. Although many fluorescent inorganic nanomaterials, such as semiconductor quantum dots, carbon dots, Ln ions dope nanoparticles etc. have been explored for biological imaging applications, these fluorescent inorganic nanomaterials are not very suitable for practical biological imaging owing to the poor degradability, renowned toxicity and poor designability [44,52–60]. Therefore, the development of fluorescent organic nanomaterials has become one of the research hotspots recently. In this report, we utilized β-CD as bridge to link AIE-active dyes with PEG and fabricated the FONs with intense green fluorescence and great biocompatibility. The successful synthesis of β-CD-NH2, TPE-CHO, TPE-Ad, TPE-β-CD-PEG was evidenced by the 1H NMR, 13C NMR and MS (Fig. S3–11). The successful fabrication of FONs with AIE feature could be confirmed by 1H NMR spectra (Fig. 1 ). It was obvious that the peaks of benzene around 6.8–8ppm and the chemical shifts of adamantane around 2ppm appeared in TPE-Ad, which could give us direct evidence that the successful synthesis of AIE dyes (TPE-Ad). On the other hand, the peaks of β-CD and PEG around 3–5ppm in β-CD-PEG could confirm that PEGylation β-CD have been prepared. Compared with the β-CD-PEG, a series of signals with peaks around 6.8–8ppm and 2ppm attributed to benzene and adamantine were found in the sample of TPE-β-CD-PEG. The difference between the β-CD-PEG and TPE-β-CD-PEG clearly suggested that β-CD-PEG was exactly encapsulated with TPE-Ad through the formation of 1:1 supramolecular complex successfully. Therefore the results of 1H NMR spectra were strong evidence to successful prepare TPE-β-CD-PEG FONs via utilizing supramolecular chemistry.

TEM image was utilized to characterize the size and morphology of TPE-β-CD-PEG FONs. As shown in Fig. S12, the diameter of spherical nanoparticles was mainly distributed in the range of 80–150nm. The formation of nanospheres could be mainly attributed to self-assembly of TPE-β-CD-PEG in aqueous solution. In other word, the self-assembly of TPE-β-CD-PEG also implied the successful formation of amphiphilic TPE-β-CD-PEG through the supramolecular inclusion interactions. During the self-assembly procedure, the hydrophobic TPE-Ad was aggregated in interior as the core, which will result in strong fluorescent emission due to the AIE feature of TPE. However, the hydrophilic polymers (β-CD-PEG) as a shell covered the hydrophobic AIE dye, which could endow the well water dispersity and great biocompatibility of TPE-β-CD-PEG FONs. The chemical structural information of TPE-β-CD-PEG and its intermediates was confirmed further characterized by FT-IR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 2 , it was obvious that a distinct peak located at 1722cm−1 in TPE-Ad, which were belonged to the stretch vibration of CO. The results could convincingly demonstrate that adamantine was successfully linked with TPE-CHO. Compared with the spectrum of TPE-Ad, peaks of CH at 2922cm−1 and peaks O of 1100cm−1 could be clearly observed in TPE-β-CD-PEG. These characteristic peaks of PEG molecule provided additional evidence for the formation of TPE-β-CD-PEG via host-guest interaction. Thus, all the results of above could confirm the successful formation of TPE-β-CD-PEG FONs.

The UV absorption spectrum of TPE-β-CD-PEG FONs in water is shown in Fig. 3 . Two adsorption peaks at 236 and 324nm can be observed in the UV–Vis spectrum of TPE-β-CD-PEG FONs. It could conclude that the adsorption peak at 236nm was the π→π * transition of CC and aromatic rings. On the other hand, the adsorption peak at 324nm can be assigned to the n→π* transition of CO. Furthermore, the self-assembly of TPE-β-CD-PEG into nanoparticles was also evidenced by UV–Vis spectroscopy. It can be seen that the UV–Vis spectrum of TPE-β-CD-PEG was escalated from 800nm. The formation of nanoparticles could result in the increase of adsorption intensity in UV–Vis spectrum. This reason can be mainly explained by the Mie theory. Meanwhile, due to the hydrophilic PEG was covered on the surface of TPE-β-CD-PEG FONs, PE-β-CD-PEG FONs showed good dispersity in complete aqueous solution. As shown in the inset of Fig. 3, we could observe the “AIE” symbol as background. The zeta potential value of TPE-β-CD-PEG FONs in water is −27.15±3.7mV based on DLS measurement, which further evidenced the good water dispersity of TPE-β-CD-PEG FONs. The excellent water dispersity of TPE-β-CD-PEG FONs indicated the great potential for biomedical applications. Moreover, the successful formation of nanoparticles was also supported by DLS measurement. We demonstrated that the hydrodynamic diameter of TPE-β-CD-PEG FONs in water is 271.87±93.8nm with a polydispersity index (PDI) of 0.212 (Fig. S13). The above results also indicated that TPE-β-CD-PEG FONs are successfully obtained through the supramolecular interaction and can readily self-assemble into nanoparticles with well dispersity. The water dispersity of TPE-β-CD-PEG FONs in phosphate buffer saline was also examined to evaluate their stability in physiological conditions. The results showed that uniform fluorescence can be found after the suspensions of TPE-β-CD-PEG FONs were deposited in PBS for 6h and were irradiated with UV lamp at 365nm (Fig. S14–15).

The fluorescence properties of TPE-β-CD-PEG FONs would be confirmed by the PL spectra (Fig. 4 ). It was noteworthy that the maximum excitation wavelength of TPE-β-CD-PEG FONs located at 323nm, while their maximum emission wavelength located at 475nm. More importantly, fluorescence quantum yield was another indispensable fluorescent property, which could reach up to 26.4% using quinine sulfate as the standard via calculating. The insert of Fig. 4 is the photography of TPE-β-CD-PEG FONs that were dispersed in pure aqueous solution. The uniform and intensive cyan-blue fluorescence can be observed after TPE-β-CD-PEG water suspension was irradiated by UV lamp at 365nm. The uniform fluorescence also indicated that TPE-β-CD-PEG FONs with amphiphilic properties would self-assemble and aggregate into core-shell micelles in aqueous solution. Moreover, the photostability of TPE-β-CD-PEG FONs was evaluated through the continuously irradiated the water suspension of TPE-β-CD-PEG FONs for 1h. As shown in Fig. S16, the fluorescent intensity of TPE-β-CD-PEG FONs was only decreased very few. The above results indicated that TPE-β-CD-PEG FONs possess excellent photostability.

The cell viability was determined using CCK-8 assay to evaluate the potential biomedical applications of TPE-β-CD-PEG FONs by cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) assay. The cell viability results of TPE-β-CD-PEG FONs towards A549 cells were displayed in Fig. 5 . It can be seen that no cell viability decrease was found after A549 cells were incubated with 10–100μgmL−1 of TPE-β-CD-PEG FONs for 8 and 24h. More interestingly, the cell viability values were even increased with the increase of incubation concentrations. This is possibly ascribed to the light adsorption and light scattering of TPE-β-CD-PEG FONs in cells. The other possible reason is the uptake of nanoparticles may induce the cell division, which may also attribute to the increase cell viability. According to the above results, we could conclude that TPE-β-CD-PEG FONs with good biocompatibility have highly potential for cell imaging.

Based on all the aforementioned characterization, we could conclude the successful fabrication of TPE-β-CD-PEG FONs with intensive green fluorescence, uniform nanoscale size, favorable dispersibility in water and excellent biocompatibility. These features endow TPE-β-CD-PEG FONs great potential for biomedical application. Therefore, the cell uptake behavior of TPE-β-CD-PEG FONs was further evaluated by confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) observation. As displayed in Fig. 6 , strong green fluorescence could be observed in cell which has been incubated with 30μgmL−1 of TPE-β-CD-PEG FONs for 3h. Furthermore, the other areas in the cell emit weak fluorescence intensity, which might be the possible locations of the cell nuclei (Fig. 6A). According to these results, we could speculate that TPE-β-CD-PEG FONs are majorly distributed in the cytoplasm. The mechanism of cell uptake pathway of TPE-β-CD-PEG FONs should be through the non-specific endocytosis of cells. More importantly, the excellent biocompatibility of the TPE-β-CD-PEG FONs would be verified further by the bright-field images (Fig. 6B). The cells still keep their natural size and morphology and well adhered to the cell plates. All the phenomenon of cell imaging could indicate their excellent staining performance, which further displayed the potential application of TPE-β-CD-PEG FONs in biomedical applications.

In summary, based on AIE-active dyes and supramolecular chemistry, the AIE-active FONs with intensive green fluorescence, uniform size, favorable water dispersity and great biocompatibility have been facilely prepared via utilizing β-CD as the bridge to link the AIE-active dye (TPE-Ad) with PEG. The characterization results convincingly demonstrated the successful preparation of TPE-β-CD-PEG FONs with excellent properties. Furthermore, the hydrophobic AIE-active dye (TPE-Ad) was encapsulated into the interior as the hydrophobic core and hydrophilic polymers were covered the surface of hydrophobic core and served as the shell. More importantly, the outstanding performance of TPE-β-CD-PEG FONs in biocompatibility evaluation and cell imaging could endow their potential for biomedical applications. Compared with traditional methods to prepare AIE-active FONs, this method is rather simple, facile treatment (stirring at room temperature and no catalyst), high atom economy (none by-product), short fabrication time (<30s) and high yield. Considering all these advantages of this method, utilizing β-CD as bridge to link AIE dyes with PEG was a creative idea and expected to be a general method for preparation of different multifunctional AIE-active polymer nanoparticles for various applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Science Foundation of China (Nos. 51363016, 21474057, 21564006, 21561022, 21644014), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangxi Province in China (Nos. 20161BAB203072, 20161BAB213066) and the National 973 Project (Nos. 2011CB935700).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2017.04.080.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: N. Yu, G. Li, Y. Gao, X. Liu, S. Ma, Int. J. Biol. Macromol.
